expanded_description: A comprehensive exploration of intermolecular forces—the attractive forces between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, melting point, viscosity, and solubility. Understanding these forces explains why substances behave differently despite similar molecular formulas.

core_concept.principle: Intermolecular forces (IMFs) are attractive forces between molecules that are weaker than chemical bonds but determine physical properties; strength increases from London dispersion < dipole-dipole < hydrogen bonding.
core_concept.explanation: While chemical bonds (ionic, covalent, metallic) hold atoms together within molecules or compounds, intermolecular forces hold molecules together in liquids and solids. These forces are much weaker than chemical bonds (typically 1-40 kJ/mol vs. 200-1000 kJ/mol for bonds) but are crucial for determining physical properties. Stronger IMFs result in higher boiling points, higher melting points, greater viscosity, and lower vapor pressure. The three main types are London dispersion forces (present in all molecules), dipole-dipole forces (present in polar molecules), and hydrogen bonding (special strong dipole-dipole interaction in molecules with H bonded to N, O, or F).

image.alt: Diagram comparing strength of different intermolecular forces
image.caption: Intermolecular force strength: London dispersion (weakest) < dipole-dipole < hydrogen bonding (strongest IMF).

sections.introduction: Before studying intermolecular forces, it's essential to distinguish them from the chemical bonds that hold atoms together within molecules. This distinction explains why water molecules stay intact when water boils (intramolecular bonds remain) but separate from each other (intermolecular forces break).

key_concept.definition: Intramolecular forces are chemical bonds within molecules (ionic, covalent, metallic); intermolecular forces are attractions between separate molecules (London dispersion, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding).
key_concept.context: Breaking intramolecular forces requires chemical reactions (high energy); breaking intermolecular forces requires physical changes like melting or boiling (lower energy).

principles.statement: Intramolecular forces (chemical bonds) are 10-100× stronger than intermolecular forces; this explains why boiling water doesn't decompose it into H₂ and O₂.
principles.application: Explains why physical changes (melting, boiling) require less energy than chemical changes (decomposition); predicts relative energy requirements for different processes.
principles.example: H₂O: O-H covalent bond energy = 463 kJ/mol (intramolecular); H-bond between water molecules = 20 kJ/mol (intermolecular). Boiling breaks H-bonds, not O-H bonds.
principles.analogy: Like a book: intramolecular forces = glue holding pages together (strong); intermolecular forces = friction between books on a shelf (weak). Removing a book (boiling) doesn't tear pages (decompose).

image.alt: Diagram comparing intramolecular bonds within molecules vs. intermolecular forces between molecules
image.caption: Intramolecular vs. intermolecular: bonds within molecules are much stronger than forces between molecules.

principles.statement: Boiling point, melting point, viscosity, surface tension, and vapor pressure all depend on intermolecular force strength, not intramolecular bond strength.
principles.application: Predicts physical properties from molecular structure; explains why similar-sized molecules have different boiling points based on polarity.
principles.example: H₂O (bp 100°C) vs. H₂S (bp -60°C): similar molar mass, but H₂O has H-bonding (strong IMF) while H₂S only has dipole-dipole (weaker IMF).
principles.analogy: Like social groups: how tightly a group sticks together (IMFs) determines how hard it is to separate members (boiling point), not how strongly individuals are built (intramolecular bonds).

image.alt: Chart showing relationship between IMF strength and physical properties
image.caption: IMF strength determines physical properties: stronger IMFs = higher bp, mp, viscosity; lower vapor pressure.

principles.principle: All Molecules Have London Dispersion Forces
principles.statement: London dispersion forces exist in all molecules (polar and nonpolar) because temporary dipoles form from momentary electron distribution fluctuations.
principles.application: Explains why nonpolar molecules can liquefy and solidify; why larger molecules have higher boiling points even if nonpolar.
principles.example: He (bp -269°C) vs. Xe (bp -108°C): both nonpolar, only London forces; Xe larger → more electrons → stronger London forces → higher bp.
principles.analogy: Like temporary handshakes: even strangers (nonpolar molecules) briefly touch hands (temporary dipoles) when passing; more people (larger molecules) = more brief contacts.

image.alt: Diagram showing temporary dipole formation in nonpolar molecules
image.caption: London dispersion: temporary dipoles form in all molecules from electron fluctuations.

components.definition: Intermolecular forces are weaker than chemical bonds but determine physical state and properties
components.interpretation: IMF strength typically 1-40 kJ/mol; covalent/ionic bonds 200-1000 kJ/mol. Physical changes break IMFs; chemical changes break bonds.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Energy Requirements for Physical vs. Chemical Changes
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the energy required to: (a) boil 1 mol of water (break IMFs), (b) decompose 1 mol of water into H₂ and O₂ (break bonds). Given: ΔH_vap = 40.7 kJ/mol, O-H bond energy = 463 kJ/mol.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate energy to boil water (break H-bonds between molecules).
step_by_step.calculation: ΔH_vap = 40.7 kJ/mol (energy to overcome IMFs)

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate energy to decompose water (break O-H bonds within molecules).
step_by_step.calculation: 2 O-H bonds per H₂O × 463 kJ/mol = 926 kJ/mol (energy to break intramolecular bonds)
Decomposition (926 kJ/mol) ÷ Boiling (40.7 kJ/mol) = 22.7× more energy for chemical change
Breaking chemical bonds requires ~23× more energy than breaking intermolecular forces

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Boiling 1 mol H₂O: 40.7 kJ (breaks H-bonds between molecules). (b) Decomposing 1 mol H₂O: 926 kJ (breaks 2 O-H bonds per molecule). Chemical changes require ~23× more energy than physical changes because intramolecular bonds are much stronger than intermolecular forces. This explains why water boils at 100°C but doesn't decompose until much higher temperatures.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Energy difference between intermolecular forces and chemical bonds

sections.section_title: London Dispersion Forces (Van der Waals Forces)
sections.introduction: London dispersion forces are the weakest intermolecular force but are present in ALL molecules, polar and nonpolar. They result from temporary fluctuations in electron distribution that create instantaneous dipoles, which induce dipoles in neighboring molecules.

key_concept.definition: London dispersion forces are temporary attractive forces that occur when electron distribution in a molecule becomes asymmetrical, creating a temporary dipole that induces dipoles in neighboring molecules.
key_concept.context: Also called van der Waals forces or induced dipole-induced dipole forces; strength increases with molecular size and surface area; only IMF in nonpolar molecules.

classifications.value: Weak London forces due to few electrons and small surface area
classifications.behavior: Few electrons → weak temporary dipoles → weak London forces → low boiling points; typically gases at room temperature.

examples.explanation: He has only 2 electrons → very weak London forces → bp = -269°C (4 K). Lowest boiling point of any element; remains liquid only near absolute zero.

image.alt: Helium atoms showing weak London dispersion forces
image.caption: Helium: only 2 electrons, weakest London forces, lowest boiling point.

examples.explanation: CH₄ has 10 electrons, nonpolar → only London forces → bp = -161°C. Gas at room temperature despite larger size than He.

image.alt: Methane molecules showing London dispersion forces
image.caption: Methane: nonpolar, only London forces, gas at room temperature.

classifications.value: Stronger London forces due to many electrons and large surface area
characteristics: Liquids or solids at room temperature
classifications.behavior: Many electrons → stronger temporary dipoles → stronger London forces → higher boiling points; can be liquids or solids at room temperature.

examples.explanation: C₈H₁₈ has 74 electrons, nonpolar → stronger London forces → bp = 125°C. Liquid at room temperature due to larger size and more electrons than methane.

image.alt: Octane molecules showing stronger London forces
image.caption: Octane: larger nonpolar molecule, stronger London forces, liquid at room temperature.

examples.explanation: I₂ has 106 electrons, nonpolar → strong London forces → mp = 114°C, bp = 184°C. Solid at room temperature despite being nonpolar.

image.alt: Iodine crystals showing strong London forces
image.caption: Iodine: large nonpolar molecule, strong London forces, solid at room temperature.

classifications.value: Linear molecules have stronger London forces than branched molecules of same mass
classifications.behavior: Linear shape → more surface area contact → stronger London forces → higher boiling point than branched isomers.

examples.explanation: Both C₅H₁₂ (72 g/mol). n-Pentane (linear): bp = 36°C. Neopentane (branched): bp = 10°C. Linear shape has more surface contact → stronger London forces.

image.alt: Comparison of linear vs. branched pentane showing surface area difference
image.caption: Molecular shape effect: linear molecules have more surface contact → stronger London forces.

principles.statement: Larger atoms/molecules with more electrons are more polarizable (electron cloud more easily distorted), creating stronger temporary dipoles and stronger London forces.
principles.application: Predicts boiling point trends in homologous series; explains why I₂ is solid while F₂ is gas despite both being nonpolar diatomic molecules.
principles.example: Halogens: F₂ (38 g/mol, bp -188°C) < Cl₂ (71 g/mol, bp -34°C) < Br₂ (160 g/mol, bp 59°C) < I₂ (254 g/mol, bp 184°C). Increasing electrons = stronger London forces.
principles.analogy: Like a cloud: small cloud (few electrons) is hard to distort; large cloud (many electrons) is easily distorted, creating stronger temporary charges.

image.alt: Diagram showing polarizability increasing with molecular size
image.caption: Polarizability: larger molecules have more easily distorted electron clouds → stronger London forces.

principles.principle: Surface Area and London Force Strength
principles.statement: Molecules with greater surface area have more contact points for London forces, resulting in stronger intermolecular attraction and higher boiling points.
principles.application: Explains why linear alkanes have higher boiling points than branched isomers; why flat molecules stack better than spherical ones.
principles.example: n-Pentane (linear, bp 36°C) vs. neopentane (spherical, bp 10°C): same formula, different shape, different surface area, different bp.
principles.analogy: Like Velcro: more surface contact (larger surface area) = more hooks and loops engaged = stronger attachment.

image.alt: Comparison of surface area contact in linear vs. branched molecules
image.caption: Surface area effect: more contact area = stronger London forces.

components.definition: London dispersion force strength depends on polarizability and surface area
components.interpretation: More electrons = more polarizable = stronger London forces. Larger surface area = more contact points = stronger London forces.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Boiling Points from London Forces
worked_examples.problem: Rank the following nonpolar molecules in order of increasing boiling point: CH₄, C₃H₈, C₅H₁₂, C₈H₁₈. Explain your reasoning based on London dispersion forces.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify that all molecules are nonpolar alkanes with only London forces.
step_by_step.calculation: All are CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ alkanes, nonpolar → only London dispersion forces present
CH₄: 10 e⁻; C₃H₈: 26 e⁻; C₅H₁₂: 42 e⁻; C₈H₁₈: 74 e⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Apply London force trend (more electrons = stronger forces = higher bp).
step_by_step.calculation: London force strength: CH₄ < C₃H₈ < C₅H₁₂ < C₈H₁₈
bp order: CH₄ (-161°C) < C₃H₈ (-42°C) < C₅H₁₂ (36°C) < C₈H₁₈ (125°C)

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing boiling point: CH₄ < C₃H₈ < C₅H₁₂ < C₈H₁₈. All are nonpolar with only London forces. Larger molecules have more electrons → more polarizable → stronger London forces → higher boiling points. This trend is consistent across all homologous series of nonpolar compounds.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using molecular size and electron count to predict London force strength and boiling points

sections.introduction: Dipole-dipole forces occur between polar molecules that have permanent dipole moments. These forces are stronger than London dispersion forces (for similar-sized molecules) because they involve attraction between permanent partial charges rather than temporary dipoles.

key_concept.definition: Dipole-dipole forces are attractive forces between the positive end (δ⁺) of one polar molecule and the negative end (δ⁻) of another polar molecule.
key_concept.context: Present only in polar molecules; stronger than London forces for similar-sized molecules; strength depends on dipole moment magnitude; molecules align to maximize attraction.

classifications.value: Moderate dipole-dipole forces with relatively low boiling points
classifications.behavior: Polar molecules align with opposite charges facing; stronger attraction than nonpolar molecules of similar size.

examples.explanation: HCl is polar (ΔEN = 0.96): H is δ⁺, Cl is δ⁻. Dipole-dipole forces between molecules → bp = -85°C. Higher than nonpolar F₂ (bp -188°C) despite similar mass.

image.alt: HCl molecules showing dipole-dipole alignment
image.caption: HCl: polar molecules align δ⁺ to δ⁻, dipole-dipole forces.

examples.explanation: H₂S is polar (bent geometry): bp = -60°C. Higher than nonpolar CH₄ (bp -161°C) despite similar molar mass (34 vs. 16 g/mol) due to dipole-dipole forces.

image.alt: H₂S molecules showing dipole-dipole alignment
image.caption: H₂S: polar bent molecule, dipole-dipole forces increase bp vs. nonpolar molecules.

classifications.value: Stronger dipole-dipole forces with higher boiling points
classifications.behavior: Larger polar molecules have stronger dipole-dipole forces plus stronger London forces; often liquids at room temperature.

examples.explanation: Acetone has polar C=O bond (ΔEN = 0.89): bp = 56°C. Liquid at room temperature due to dipole-dipole forces plus London forces from larger size.

image.alt: Acetone molecules showing dipole-dipole alignment
image.caption: Acetone: polar C=O bond creates dipole-dipole forces, liquid at room temperature.

examples.explanation: CH₂Cl₂ is polar (asymmetric): bp = 40°C. Two polar C-Cl bonds create significant dipole moment → dipole-dipole forces → liquid at room temperature.

image.alt: CH₂Cl₂ molecules showing dipole-dipole alignment
image.caption: CH₂Cl₂: asymmetric polar molecule, dipole-dipole forces.

principles.statement: Larger dipole moments create stronger dipole-dipole forces; dipole moment (μ) depends on charge separation and distance: μ = Q × r.
principles.application: Predicts relative boiling points of polar molecules; explains why molecules with larger ΔEN have higher boiling points.
principles.example: HCl (μ = 1.08 D, bp -85°C) vs. HBr (μ = 0.82 D, bp -66°C): HCl has larger dipole but HBr has more electrons; competing effects.
principles.analogy: Like magnets: stronger magnets (larger dipole moment) attract more strongly than weaker magnets.

image.alt: Diagram showing relationship between dipole moment and force strength
image.caption: Dipole moment: larger charge separation = stronger dipole-dipole forces.

principles.statement: Polar molecules align in liquids and solids to maximize δ⁺ to δ⁻ attraction and minimize δ⁺ to δ⁺ or δ⁻ to δ⁻ repulsion.
principles.application: Explains molecular arrangement in polar liquids and solids; affects crystal structure and physical properties.
principles.example: Solid HCl: molecules align in chains with H(δ⁺) facing Cl(δ⁻) of neighboring molecule. This ordered arrangement maximizes attraction.

image.alt: Diagram showing polar molecule alignment in dipole-dipole interactions
image.caption: Molecular alignment: polar molecules align δ⁺ to δ⁻ to maximize attraction.

components.definition: Dipole-dipole force strength depends on dipole moment magnitude
components.equation: $\text{Dipole-Dipole Strength} \propto \mu^2 \propto (Q \times r)^2$ where μ = dipole moment
components.interpretation: Larger dipole moment = stronger dipole-dipole forces. Dipole moment increases with greater electronegativity difference and bond length.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Dipole-Dipole and London Forces
worked_examples.problem: Compare the boiling points of: (a) CO (polar, 28 g/mol, bp -191°C), (b) N₂ (nonpolar, 28 g/mol, bp -196°C). Explain the difference based on intermolecular forces.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the intermolecular forces present in each molecule.
step_by_step.calculation: CO: polar → dipole-dipole + London forces; N₂: nonpolar → London forces only

step_by_step.explanation: Compare molar masses (affects London force strength).
step_by_step.calculation: CO: 28 g/mol; N₂: 28 g/mol → same mass → similar London force strength
CO: bp -191°C; N₂: bp -196°C → CO is 5°C higher
CO has dipole-dipole forces in addition to London forces; N₂ has only London forces → CO has stronger total IMFs → higher bp

worked_examples.final_answer: CO has a higher boiling point (-191°C) than N₂ (-196°C) despite identical molar masses (28 g/mol). Both have similar London forces due to same mass, but CO is polar (has dipole-dipole forces) while N₂ is nonpolar (London forces only). The additional dipole-dipole forces in CO require more energy to overcome, resulting in a 5°C higher boiling point. This demonstrates the effect of polarity on physical properties.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comparing dipole-dipole vs. London forces using molecules of similar mass

sections.introduction: Hydrogen bonding is a special, unusually strong type of dipole-dipole interaction that occurs when hydrogen is bonded to highly electronegative atoms (N, O, or F). Despite being called a 'bond,' hydrogen bonding is an intermolecular force, not a chemical bond, but it's strong enough to significantly affect physical properties.

key_concept.definition: Hydrogen bonding is a strong dipole-dipole attraction between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to N, O, or F (δ⁺) and a lone pair on N, O, or F (δ⁻) of another molecule.
key_concept.context: Requires H bonded to N, O, or F (very electronegative); strongest intermolecular force (10-40 kJ/mol); explains water's unique properties, DNA structure, and protein folding.

classifications.behavior: Molecules form networks through H-bonds; requires more energy to separate molecules → higher boiling points.

examples.explanation: Each H₂O can form up to 4 H-bonds (2 H donors, 2 lone pair acceptors). Extensive H-bonding network → bp = 100°C (anomalously high for 18 g/mol). Ice less dense than liquid due to H-bond network.

image.alt: Water molecules showing hydrogen bonding network
image.caption: Water: each molecule forms up to 4 H-bonds, creating extensive network.

examples.explanation: Each NH₃ can form up to 2 H-bonds (3 H donors, 1 lone pair acceptor, limited by lone pairs). H-bonding → bp = -33°C (higher than PH₃ bp -88°C despite PH₃ being heavier).

image.alt: Ammonia molecules showing hydrogen bonding
image.caption: Ammonia: H-bonding increases bp compared to non-H-bonding PH₃.

examples.explanation: Each HF can form up to 2 H-bonds (1 H donor, 3 lone pair acceptors, limited by H). Strongest H-bonds (F most electronegative) → bp = 20°C (highest of hydrogen halides).

image.alt: HF molecules showing hydrogen bonding chains
image.caption: HF: strongest H-bonds due to F's high electronegativity.

classifications.value: Hydrogen bonds within the same molecule
classifications.behavior: H-bond within molecule creates stable ring structure; can reduce intermolecular H-bonding → lower boiling point than expected.

examples.explanation: OH and NO₂ groups adjacent on benzene ring form intramolecular H-bond. Creates 6-membered ring. Lower bp (214°C) than p-nitrophenol (279°C) which has intermolecular H-bonds.

image.alt: o-Nitrophenol showing intramolecular hydrogen bond
image.caption: Intramolecular H-bond: within molecule, reduces intermolecular forces.

examples.explanation: α-helices and β-sheets stabilized by intramolecular H-bonds between backbone N-H and C=O groups. Critical for protein folding and function.

image.alt: Protein α-helix showing intramolecular hydrogen bonds
image.caption: Protein structure: intramolecular H-bonds stabilize α-helices and β-sheets.

classifications.value: Critical H-bonds in DNA, proteins, and other biomolecules
classifications.behavior: H-bonds provide specificity (A-T, G-C pairing) and reversibility (DNA replication, protein folding) essential for biological function.

examples.explanation: A-T pairs have 2 H-bonds; G-C pairs have 3 H-bonds. Specific H-bonding ensures accurate DNA replication and transcription. G-C pairs stronger (3 H-bonds) → higher melting temperature.

image.alt: DNA base pairs showing hydrogen bonding
image.caption: DNA: H-bonds between base pairs enable specific pairing and replication.

examples.explanation: H-bonds between backbone atoms and side chains stabilize protein 3D structure. Denaturation breaks H-bonds, unfolding protein and destroying function.

image.alt: Protein structure showing hydrogen bonding
image.caption: Protein folding: H-bonds stabilize 3D structure essential for function.

principles.statement: Hydrogen bonding requires: (1) H covalently bonded to N, O, or F (creates large δ⁺ on H), (2) Lone pair on N, O, or F in another molecule (δ⁻ acceptor).
principles.application: Predicts which molecules can H-bond; explains why CH₄ doesn't H-bond (no N, O, F) while H₂O does; why HCl doesn't H-bond (Cl not electronegative enough).
principles.example: H₂O: H bonded to O ✓, O has lone pairs ✓ → H-bonding. CH₄: H bonded to C ✗ (C not electronegative enough) → no H-bonding. HF: H bonded to F ✓, F has lone pairs ✓ → H-bonding.
principles.analogy: Like a lock and key: H (key) must fit with N/O/F lone pair (lock); wrong key (H bonded to C, Cl, etc.) doesn't work.

image.alt: Diagram showing hydrogen bonding requirements
image.caption: H-bond requirements: H bonded to N/O/F + lone pair on N/O/F.

principles.statement: H-bond strength increases with electronegativity of the atom bonded to H: F-H···F > O-H···O > N-H···N.
principles.application: Explains why HF has strongest H-bonds; why water has moderate H-bonds; why ammonia has weaker H-bonds.
principles.example: H-bond energies: F-H···F (40 kJ/mol) > O-H···O (20 kJ/mol) > N-H···N (10 kJ/mol). F most electronegative → largest δ⁺ on H → strongest H-bond.

image.alt: Comparison of H-bond strengths for F, O, and N
image.caption: H-bond strength: F-H > O-H > N-H due to electronegativity differences.

principles.statement: Maximum H-bonds per molecule depends on number of H donors (H bonded to N/O/F) and H acceptors (lone pairs on N/O/F).
principles.application: Predicts extent of H-bonding network; explains why water (4 H-bonds max) has higher bp than HF (2 H-bonds max) despite HF having stronger individual H-bonds.
principles.example: H₂O: 2 H donors + 2 lone pair acceptors = 4 H-bonds max. NH₃: 3 H donors + 1 lone pair = 2 H-bonds max (limited by lone pairs). HF: 1 H donor + 3 lone pairs = 2 H-bonds max (limited by H).

image.alt: Diagram showing maximum H-bonds for H₂O, NH₃, and HF
image.caption: H-bonds per molecule: H₂O (4) > NH₃ (2) = HF (2) due to donor/acceptor balance.

components.interpretation: H-bonding requires H bonded to N, O, or F. Strength increases with electronegativity. Number of H-bonds depends on donors and acceptors available.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Hydrogen Bonding Capability
worked_examples.problem: Determine which of the following molecules can form hydrogen bonds: (a) CH₃OH, (b) CH₃OCH₃, (c) CH₄, (d) HF. Explain your reasoning for each.

step_by_step.calculation: Has O-H bond (H bonded to O) ✓; O has lone pairs ✓ → CAN form H-bonds
No O-H bond (H bonded to C, not O) ✗; O has lone pairs but no H donor → CANNOT form H-bonds with itself (can accept from other molecules)
No N, O, or F ✗; H bonded to C (not electronegative enough) → CANNOT form H-bonds
Has H-F bond (H bonded to F) ✓; F has lone pairs ✓ → CAN form H-bonds

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) CH₃OH: CAN form H-bonds (has O-H bond). (b) CH₃OCH₃: CANNOT form H-bonds with itself (no O-H bond, only C-H bonds). (c) CH₄: CANNOT form H-bonds (no N, O, or F). (d) HF: CAN form H-bonds (has H-F bond). H-bonding requires H covalently bonded to N, O, or F; molecules without this cannot H-bond with themselves.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying hydrogen bonding capability from molecular structure

worked_examples.title: Explaining Boiling Point Anomalies with Hydrogen Bonding
worked_examples.problem: Explain why H₂O (bp 100°C) has a much higher boiling point than H₂S (bp -60°C), H₂Se (bp -41°C), and H₂Te (bp -2°C) despite being the lightest molecule.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify intermolecular forces in each molecule.
step_by_step.calculation: H₂O: H-bonding + dipole-dipole + London; H₂S/Se/Te: dipole-dipole + London only (S, Se, Te not electronegative enough for H-bonding)
Without H-bonding: bp should increase with mass (more electrons = stronger London): H₂O < H₂S < H₂Se < H₂Te
Actual: H₂O (100°C) >> H₂Te (-2°C) > H₂Se (-41°C) > H₂S (-60°C). H₂O is 100+°C higher than expected!
H₂O has H-bonding (O is very electronegative); H₂S/Se/Te don't (S, Se, Te not electronegative enough). H-bonds in water require much more energy to break.

worked_examples.final_answer: H₂O has an anomalously high boiling point (100°C) compared to H₂S (-60°C), H₂Se (-41°C), and H₂Te (-2°C) because only H₂O can form hydrogen bonds. Oxygen is electronegative enough to create strong H-bonds (O-H···O), while S, Se, and Te are not. Without H-bonding, boiling points would increase with molecular mass (H₂O < H₂S < H₂Se < H₂Te), but H-bonding in water overrides this trend, requiring much more energy to separate molecules. This demonstrates the dramatic effect of hydrogen bonding on physical properties.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using hydrogen bonding to explain boiling point anomalies in Group 16 hydrides

sections.introduction: Different types of intermolecular forces have different strengths, and molecules often experience multiple types simultaneously. Understanding the relative strengths helps predict which forces dominate and how they affect physical properties.

key_concept.definition: Intermolecular force strength ranking: London dispersion (0.05-40 kJ/mol) < dipole-dipole (5-25 kJ/mol) < hydrogen bonding (10-40 kJ/mol) << covalent/ionic bonds (200-1000 kJ/mol).
key_concept.context: Most molecules experience multiple IMF types; strongest type dominates physical properties; larger molecules have stronger London forces that can exceed dipole-dipole in smaller molecules.

principles.statement: IMF strength ranges overlap; large nonpolar molecules can have stronger London forces than small polar molecules have dipole-dipole forces.
principles.application: Explains why large nonpolar molecules (I₂, bp 184°C) have higher boiling points than small polar molecules (HCl, bp -85°C); size matters as much as polarity.
principles.example: I₂ (nonpolar, 254 g/mol, bp 184°C) vs. HCl (polar, 36.5 g/mol, bp -85°C): I₂'s strong London forces (large size) exceed HCl's dipole-dipole forces.
principles.analogy: Like comparing athletes: a large nonpolar molecule (strong London) can be 'stronger' than a small polar molecule (dipole-dipole) despite different 'sports' (force types).

image.alt: Chart showing overlapping strength ranges of different IMF types
image.caption: IMF strength overlap: large nonpolar molecules can have stronger London forces than small polar molecules' dipole-dipole.

principles.statement: The strongest intermolecular force present in a molecule dominates its physical properties; identify the strongest IMF to predict behavior.
principles.application: Predicts boiling points, solubility, and other properties; H-bonding dominates when present; otherwise dipole-dipole or London forces.
principles.example: CH₃CH₂OH (ethanol): has H-bonding, dipole-dipole, London → H-bonding dominates → bp 78°C. CH₃OCH₃ (dimethyl ether): dipole-dipole + London only → bp -24°C. Same formula, different IMFs, very different bp.

image.alt: Diagram showing how dominant IMF determines properties
image.caption: Dominant IMF: strongest force present determines physical properties.

principles.statement: Molecules experience all applicable IMFs simultaneously; total intermolecular attraction is the sum of all forces present.
principles.application: Explains why polar molecules with H-bonding have highest boiling points; why larger polar molecules have higher bp than smaller ones with same IMF types.
principles.example: H₂O: H-bonding (20 kJ/mol) + dipole-dipole (5 kJ/mol) + London (2 kJ/mol) ≈ 27 kJ/mol total. CH₄: London only (2 kJ/mol). H₂O has 13× stronger total IMFs.

image.alt: Diagram showing cumulative effect of multiple IMFs
image.caption: Cumulative IMFs: molecules experience all applicable forces; strongest dominates but all contribute.

components.interpretation: All molecules have London forces. Polar molecules add dipole-dipole. Molecules with H-N/O/F add H-bonding. Strongest type dominates properties.

worked_examples.title: Ranking Molecules by Intermolecular Force Strength
worked_examples.problem: Rank the following molecules in order of increasing intermolecular force strength: (a) CH₄, (b) CH₃Cl, (c) CH₃OH, (d) C₈H₁₈. Explain your ranking.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify IMFs present in each molecule.
step_by_step.calculation: CH₄: London only; CH₃Cl: London + dipole-dipole; CH₃OH: London + dipole-dipole + H-bonding; C₈H₁₈: London only (larger)
CH₄ (16 g/mol) < CH₃Cl (50 g/mol) < CH₃OH (32 g/mol) < C₈H₁₈ (114 g/mol) for London forces
CH₄: London (weak); CH₃Cl: dipole-dipole dominates; CH₃OH: H-bonding dominates; C₈H₁₈: London (strong due to size)

step_by_step.explanation: Rank by total IMF strength (verify with boiling points).
step_by_step.calculation: CH₄ (bp -161°C) < CH₃Cl (bp -24°C) < C₈H₁₈ (bp 125°C) < CH₃OH (bp 65°C). Wait, C₈H₁₈ > CH₃OH by bp! Large London can exceed H-bonding in small molecules.

worked_examples.final_answer: IMF strength ranking: CH₄ < CH₃Cl < CH₃OH < C₈H₁₈ (by boiling point). CH₄ has only weak London forces. CH₃Cl adds dipole-dipole. CH₃OH has H-bonding (strongest IMF type). However, C₈H₁₈ is so large that its London forces exceed CH₃OH's H-bonding, giving it a higher boiling point. This demonstrates that very large nonpolar molecules can have stronger total IMFs than smaller H-bonding molecules.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Ranking molecules by IMF strength considering all force types and molecular size

sections.introduction: Intermolecular forces directly determine physical properties of substances. Understanding these relationships enables prediction of boiling points, melting points, viscosity, surface tension, vapor pressure, and solubility from molecular structure.

key_concept.definition: Stronger intermolecular forces result in: higher boiling points, higher melting points, higher viscosity, higher surface tension, lower vapor pressure, and specific solubility patterns ('like dissolves like').
key_concept.context: These property relationships are fundamental to understanding phase changes, separation techniques, and material selection in chemistry and industry.

principles.statement: Boiling point increases with intermolecular force strength; more energy required to separate molecules with stronger IMFs.
principles.application: Predicts relative boiling points from molecular structure; explains trends in homologous series; guides distillation and purification.
principles.example: Alkanes: CH₄ (-161°C) < C₂H₆ (-89°C) < C₃H₈ (-42°C) < C₄H₁₀ (-0.5°C). Increasing size → stronger London forces → higher bp.

image.alt: Graph showing boiling point vs. IMF strength
image.caption: Boiling point: increases with IMF strength; more energy needed to separate molecules.

principles.statement: Viscosity (resistance to flow) increases with intermolecular force strength; stronger IMFs make molecules 'stick' together more, resisting flow.
principles.application: Explains why honey (H-bonding) is more viscous than water; why motor oil viscosity changes with temperature; guides lubricant selection.
principles.example: Water (H-bonding): viscosity 0.89 cP at 25°C. Hexane (London only): viscosity 0.30 cP at 25°C. H-bonding makes water ~3× more viscous.

image.alt: Comparison of viscosity for different liquids
image.caption: Viscosity: stronger IMFs = more resistance to flow = higher viscosity.

principles.statement: Surface tension (energy required to increase surface area) increases with intermolecular force strength; molecules at surface experience net inward pull.
principles.application: Explains water droplet formation, capillary action, insect walking on water; guides surfactant design to reduce surface tension.
principles.example: Water (H-bonding): surface tension 72 mN/m at 20°C. Ethanol (weaker H-bonding): 22 mN/m. Hexane (London only): 18 mN/m.

image.alt: Diagram showing surface tension and molecular forces
image.caption: Surface tension: stronger IMFs = stronger inward pull on surface molecules = higher surface tension.

principles.statement: Vapor pressure (pressure of vapor in equilibrium with liquid) decreases with intermolecular force strength; stronger IMFs make molecules less likely to escape to vapor phase.
principles.application: Explains why volatile liquids (weak IMFs) evaporate quickly; predicts evaporation rates; guides solvent selection for specific applications.
principles.example: Diethyl ether (weak IMFs): vapor pressure 537 mmHg at 20°C (highly volatile). Water (H-bonding): 17.5 mmHg at 20°C (less volatile).

image.alt: Graph showing inverse relationship between vapor pressure and IMF strength
image.caption: Vapor pressure: stronger IMFs = fewer molecules escape = lower vapor pressure.

principles.statement: 'Like dissolves like': polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents; nonpolar solutes dissolve in nonpolar solvents. IMF compatibility determines solubility.
principles.application: Predicts solubility; guides solvent selection for extractions and reactions; explains why oil and water don't mix.
principles.example: NaCl (ionic) dissolves in H₂O (polar) but not hexane (nonpolar). Oil (nonpolar) dissolves in hexane but not water. Matching IMFs enables mixing.

image.alt: Diagram showing solubility based on IMF compatibility
image.caption: Solubility: 'like dissolves like' - matching IMFs enables solute-solvent mixing.

components.definition: Physical property relationships with IMF strength
components.equation: $\text{Stronger IMFs} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Higher bp, mp} \\ \text{Higher viscosity} \\ \text{Higher surface tension} \\ \text{Lower vapor pressure} \\ \text{Polar in polar, nonpolar in nonpolar} \end{cases}$
components.interpretation: All physical properties listed increase (except vapor pressure) with stronger IMFs. Solubility depends on IMF matching between solute and solvent.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Physical Properties from IMF Analysis
worked_examples.problem: Compare ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH) and dimethyl ether (CH₃OCH₃). Both have formula C₂H₆O (46 g/mol). Predict which has: (a) higher boiling point, (b) higher viscosity, (c) higher vapor pressure. Explain.

step_by_step.calculation: Ethanol: has O-H bond → H-bonding + dipole-dipole + London. Dimethyl ether: no O-H bond → dipole-dipole + London only
Ethanol has H-bonding (strongest IMF); dimethyl ether doesn't. Ethanol has stronger total IMFs.

step_by_step.explanation: Predict boiling point (stronger IMFs = higher bp).
step_by_step.calculation: Ethanol: bp 78°C; Dimethyl ether: bp -24°C. Ethanol 102°C higher due to H-bonding!
Viscosity: ethanol > ether (stronger IMFs). Vapor pressure: ethanol < ether (stronger IMFs = fewer molecules escape)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Higher bp: ethanol (78°C vs. -24°C) due to H-bonding. (b) Higher viscosity: ethanol (stronger IMFs resist flow). (c) Higher vapor pressure: dimethyl ether (weaker IMFs = more molecules escape to vapor). Same molecular formula, different structures, different IMFs, dramatically different properties. This demonstrates how molecular structure determines IMFs which determine physical properties.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Predicting multiple physical properties from intermolecular force analysis

sections.introduction: Understanding intermolecular forces has practical applications in drug design, materials science, separation techniques, and everyday products. This knowledge enables rational design of molecules and materials with desired properties.

key_concept.definition: IMF knowledge enables design of molecules with specific boiling points, solubility, viscosity, and other properties for pharmaceuticals, polymers, surfactants, and industrial applications.
key_concept.context: From designing drugs that dissolve in blood to creating non-stick coatings, IMF understanding guides molecular design across chemistry disciplines.

classifications.value: Optimizing drug solubility and bioavailability through IMF control
classifications.behavior: Drugs need appropriate IMFs to dissolve in blood (aqueous, polar) and cross cell membranes (lipid, nonpolar); IMF balance critical for bioavailability.

examples.explanation: Aspirin has polar -COOH group (H-bonding with water) and nonpolar benzene ring (London forces with membranes). Balance enables oral absorption and blood transport.

image.alt: Aspirin structure showing polar and nonpolar regions
image.caption: Aspirin: polar group for water solubility, nonpolar region for membrane crossing.

examples.explanation: Controlling IMFs during crystallization affects drug purity, stability, and dissolution rate. Different crystal forms (polymorphs) have different IMFs and properties.

image.alt: Drug crystal structure showing intermolecular forces
image.caption: Drug crystallization: IMF control affects crystal form and properties.

classifications.value: Creating molecules with both polar and nonpolar regions for cleaning
classifications.behavior: Surfactants have polar heads that interact with water and nonpolar tails that interact with oil; enables mixing of oil and water for cleaning.

examples.explanation: Soap: polar carboxylate head (-COO⁻, ion-dipole with water) + nonpolar hydrocarbon tail (London forces with oil). Forms micelles that trap oil for removal.

image.alt: Soap micelle structure showing IMF interactions
image.caption: Soap: polar heads H-bond with water, nonpolar tails interact with oil via London forces.

examples.explanation: Detergents have sulfonate heads (very polar, strong ion-dipole with water) + hydrocarbon tails. More effective than soap in hard water (doesn't form precipitate).

image.alt: Detergent molecule showing polar and nonpolar regions
image.caption: Detergent: designed IMFs enable oil removal in various water conditions.

classifications.value: Separating compounds based on IMF differences
classifications.behavior: Compounds separate based on IMF strength with stationary vs. mobile phase; polar compounds stick to polar stationary phase longer.

examples.explanation: Nonpolar stationary phase + polar mobile phase. Nonpolar compounds interact more with stationary phase → elute later. Polar compounds elute first. Separates by IMF differences.

image.alt: HPLC chromatogram showing separation by polarity
image.caption: HPLC: compounds separate based on IMF differences with stationary and mobile phases.

examples.explanation: Polar paper (cellulose, H-bonding) + less polar solvent. Polar compounds stick to paper (H-bonding); nonpolar compounds move with solvent. Separates pigments, amino acids, etc.

image.alt: Paper chromatography showing separation
image.caption: Paper chromatography: polar compounds H-bond with paper, move slower.

classifications.value: Creating materials with specific properties through IMF control
classifications.behavior: Polymer properties (strength, flexibility, melting point) depend on IMFs between chains; H-bonding creates strong fibers; London forces create flexible plastics.

examples.explanation: Nylon has amide groups (-CONH-) that form H-bonds between polymer chains. H-bonding creates strong, durable fibers for clothing, ropes, and industrial applications.

image.alt: Nylon polymer chains showing intermolecular H-bonding
image.caption: Nylon: H-bonding between chains creates strong, durable material.

examples.explanation: Teflon (PTFE) has C-F bonds (very nonpolar, weak London forces). Weak IMFs with other materials → nothing sticks → non-stick cookware coating.

image.alt: Teflon surface showing weak intermolecular forces
image.caption: Teflon: weak London forces → non-stick surface.

worked_examples.title: Designing a Solvent for Specific Application
worked_examples.problem: You need to design a solvent to remove grease (nonpolar) from metal parts but also rinse away with water. What IMF characteristics should the solvent have? Give an example.

step_by_step.calculation: Grease is nonpolar → need nonpolar region in solvent (London forces) to dissolve grease
Water is polar with H-bonding → need polar region in solvent (H-bonding or dipole-dipole) to mix with water
Amphiphilic solvent: nonpolar tail (dissolves grease) + polar head (mixes with water)
Isopropanol (CH₃CHOHCH₃): nonpolar CH₃ groups (London forces with grease) + polar -OH group (H-bonding with water). Common degreaser that rinses with water.

worked_examples.final_answer: Solvent design: amphiphilic molecule with nonpolar region (London forces to dissolve grease) and polar region (H-bonding to mix with water). Example: isopropanol (rubbing alcohol) has nonpolar CH₃ groups that interact with grease via London forces and polar -OH group that H-bonds with water. This enables grease removal followed by water rinsing. IMF understanding guides solvent design for specific applications.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying IMF knowledge to design solvent for specific application

summary: Intermolecular forces (IMFs) are attractive forces between molecules that determine physical properties; weaker than chemical bonds (1-40 kJ/mol vs. 200-1000 kJ/mol) but crucial for boiling point, melting point, viscosity, surface tension, and solubility. Three main types: (1) London dispersion forces (present in ALL molecules, from temporary dipoles, strength increases with molecular size and surface area); (2) Dipole-dipole forces (present in polar molecules only, from permanent dipoles, stronger than London for similar-sized molecules); (3) Hydrogen bonding (special strong dipole-dipole when H bonded to N, O, or F, strongest IMF, explains water's unique properties). IMF strength ranking: London < dipole-dipole < H-bonding << covalent/ionic bonds. Stronger IMFs result in: higher bp/mp, higher viscosity, higher surface tension, lower vapor pressure. 'Like dissolves like': polar dissolves in polar, nonpolar in nonpolar. Applications include: pharmaceutical design (balance polar/nonpolar for bioavailability), surfactants (amphiphilic molecules for cleaning), chromatography (separation by IMF differences), polymer design (H-bonding for strength, London forces for flexibility). Understanding IMFs enables prediction of physical properties from molecular structure and rational design of materials with desired properties.
